coredata.dc:title: Development of a selective “on-off-on” nano-sensor based on lanthanide encapsulated carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiangqian
Gao, Jinwei
Rao, Shuangyue
Zheng, Yuhui
coredata.dc:description: The example of a lanthanide based hybrid nanoprobe has been developed through functionalization of carbon dots with the organic pendant arms (ethylenediaminetetraaceticdianhydride). This assay has been designed for the highly specific recognition of an anthracis biomarker (2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, DPA) in pure aqueous medium and the improved red emissions increase with the addition of DPA. In the next stage, this nanosystem demonstrates high selectivity for the determination of copper ions in terms of the switch-off effect. Furthermore, the presence of thiols such as glutathione (GSH) has induced large enhancements of the red luminescence. The corresponding detection limits are determined to be 137nM for DPA, 80nM for Cu(II) and 50nM for GSH respectively. The employments of organic solvents, separation treatments and advanced instrumentations have been ruled out during the detection processes. The luminescence changes can be realized with the bare eye observation under the irradiation of a portable UV lamp. The results reported here may open the new way for designing multiple tailor-made sensing systems by finely tuning the features of both organic moieties and carbon nanostructures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lanthanide
Sensor
Thiols

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2)
Synthesis of EDTA-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-EDTA)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CQDs-EDTA-Eu based fluorescence nanoprobe for detection of DPA and GSH.
TEM images of CQDs-NH2 (A) and CQDs-EDTA-Eu (B).

FTIR spectra of CQDS-NH2(red line), CQDs-EDTA (blue line) and CQDs-EDTA-Eu(black line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
UV–vis spectra for DPA, CQDs-NH2, CQDs-EDTA-Eu and CQDs-EDTA-Eu+DPA in aqueous solution.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs-EDTA-Eu (10μg/ml) in aqueous solution after the addition of DPA (0–10μM) (excitation wavelength at 282nm) (Inset: photographs of the CQDs-EDTA-Eu dispersion taken under a UV lamp with λ=254nm before (left) and after (right) addition of 10μM DPA).
Relative fluorescence intensity (F-F0) changed linearly with DPA concentration.

Fluorescence responses of CQDs-EDTA-Eu toward different potentially interfering amino acids or common biorelevant ions and aromatic carboxylic acids. The concentrations of all the compounds are the same (10.0μM) and measurements are performed under identical conditions.
Relative fluorescence intensity of CQDs-EDTA-Eu in the presence of Cu2+ at different concentrations.
Fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs-EDTA-Eu-DPA containing 100μM Cu2+ with increasing amounts of GSH.

head.ce:title: Development of a selective “on-off-on” nano-sensor based on lanthanide encapsulated carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Materials, Academy of Advanced Optoelectronics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The example of a lanthanide based hybrid nanoprobe has been developed through functionalization of carbon dots with the organic pendant arms (ethylenediaminetetraaceticdianhydride). This assay has been designed for the highly specific recognition of an anthracis biomarker (2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, DPA) in pure aqueous medium and the improved red emissions increase with the addition of DPA. In the next stage, this nanosystem demonstrates high selectivity for the determination of copper ions in terms of the switch-off effect. Furthermore, the presence of thiols such as glutathione (GSH) has induced large enhancements of the red luminescence. The corresponding detection limits are determined to be 137nM for DPA, 80nM for Cu(II) and 50nM for GSH respectively. The employments of organic solvents, separation treatments and advanced instrumentations have been ruled out during the detection processes. The luminescence changes can be realized with the bare eye observation under the irradiation of a portable UV lamp. The results reported here may open the new way for designing multiple tailor-made sensing systems by finely tuning the features of both organic moieties and carbon nanostructures.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacillus anthracis has aroused considerable attention in the last decades due to the lethality of its spores to humans [1]. Efficient and practical methods capable of monitoring anthracis spores are necessary to protect humans and animals from anthrax disease or bio-terrorism [2]. 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) is the major component in protective layers of bacterial spores (about 5–15% of the drymass of bacterial spores) [3]. Therefore, Bacillus anthracis spores can be detected by recognition of this unique biomarker [4]. As far as copper ion is concerned, this essential element is toxic at elevated concentrations. It is not biodegradable and is able to accumulate in organisms. Hence, the accurate measurement approaches have been developed to determine the existence of the metals [5–7]. In addition, glutathione (GSH) plays a critical role in the maintenance of biological systems [8–10].The abnormal level of GSH causes a variety of serious problems, including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and liver disease. In this case, the preparation of probe materials for the biothiols detection is real a challenge.

Carbon nanoparticles less than 10nm (carbon quantum dots, or CQDs) appear as new luminescent nanomaterials in recent years [11,12]. CQDs contain stacked sp2-hybridized carbon layers with functional groups such as carboxy-, hydoxy-, or amide at the periphery [13,14].These building blocks are well-accepted for their tunable light absorption and variable photoluminescence. Compared to traditional semiconductor based quantum dots (QDs), CQDs with excellent quantum yield, photostability, lower toxicity and biocompatibility, have been employed to develop a series of smart sensing systems for a wide range of targets, such as heavy metal ions [15–18], anions, organic pollutants [19] and biomolecules [20–23].

On the other side, lanthanide ion exhibits extraordinary spectroscopic features, including long excited states, large Stoke’s shift and sharp line-like emissions [24–28]. Lanthanide-based luminescent materials have been extensively explored in chemical and biological sensing systems due to their dynamic response to specific analytes [29–31].Therefore, we have reported the synthesis of a novel fluorescent nanoprobe (CQDs-EDTA-Eu) based on carbon quantum dots, which enables to emit in the blue region. Europium ion has been selected as the incorporated cations and would have great potential to display its unique emissions (Fig. 1 ). In the presence of DPA, the blue luminescence of CQDs-EDTA-Eu decreased significantly and converted to red color. Accordingly, DPA has been involved in coordination with europium ions to form the hybrid structure (CQDs-EDTA-Eu-DPA). Upon the addition of Cu2+, the red fluorescence has been suppressed. Finally, the titration of GSH could lead to emission recovery and the red luminescence has been quickly restored. The effective design of the optical probe as sensing operators would provide a general and facile way for the determination of various biological and chemical key species in the absence of sophisticated instruments.

Eu2O3 (99.9%) was purchased from Shanghai yuelong Company (Shanghai, China). Europium nitrate was obtained by dissolving Eu2O3 in concentrated nitric acid. Citric acid (AR), ethylenediamine (99%), ethylenediaminetetraaceticdianhydride (98%), anhydrous ethanol (AR), 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), Dopamine (DA), adrenaline (AD), aminoacids (glutamic,arginine, Tyrosine, Cysteine, Phenylalanine, Lysine, Alanine and Glycine), reduced glutathione (GSH) were provided by Sigma-Aldrich corporation. All other reagents were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2)
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-NH2 was synthesized according to literature procedure with slight modifications [32]. Citric acid (CA, 1.0g) and ethylenediamine (EDA, 1.0ml) were sufficiently dissolved and dispersed in 10ml deionized water by sonication for 30min. The mixed solution was transferred to a 25ml Teflon-lined autoclave chamber and heated to 200°C for 4h. After cooling to room temperature, the CQDs-NH2 containing solution was centrifuged at 12000rpm for 10min to remove large precipitates. For further purifying the CQDs-NH2, the resultant solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off=1000) for 3days.The CQDs-NH2 was then precipitated from the dispersion, washed twice with absolute ethanol and vacuum lyophilized for 10h. (Fig. S1)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of EDTA-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-EDTA)
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-NH2 (0.1g) was dispersed by sonication in bicarbonate buffer (10ml,0.1M) at pH=9.6. In the following step, 2∼3 drops of N,N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) as the catalyst were added in the solution. Then, 2ml of ethylenediaminetetraaceticdianhydride (EDTAD=0.1M) in DMSO was slowly added and stirred at room temperature for 48h in the darkness. CQDs-EDTA was precipitated, washed three times with anhydrous ethanol, and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off=1000) for 3days to remove the excessive EDTAD. The sample has been dried in vacuum before uses.

The CQDs-EDTA (10mg) was firstly dissolved in 5ml Tris-HCl buffer (0.05M, pH 7.4). Then, the solution was added dropwise with Eu(NO3)3 (5ml, 0.01M) by stirring at room temperature for 48h in the darkness. Finally, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off=1000) for 3days to remove residual Eu(NO3)3. The resultant sample was stored at 4°C in the refrigerator.

TEM images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope. FT-IR spectra were measured within the 4000–400cm−1 wavenumber range by using a Prestige-21 spectrometer (Shimadzu) with the KBr pellet technique. UV–vis spectra were recorded on an Agilent8453 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence and excitation spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 450W xenon lamp as light source. Zeta potential measurements were analyzed by a Nano ZS (Malvern Instrument, UK).

As for the surface modification, ethylenediaminetetraaceticdianhydride (EDTAD) was employed as the covalently-bonded organic linker to react with the achieved CQDs-NH2. It has been found that the organic structure can be attached onto the amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) and generated the carboxyl moieties appended carbon dots (CQDs-EDTA). With the aim of studying the functionalization process, we performed the zeta potential titrations at variable pH environments for the two samples (CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-EDTA) (Fig. S2). It has been found that the surface potentials with different charge density were achieved before and after the incorporation of ethylenediaminetetraaceticdianhydride. The carboxyl coating shell over the inner carbon particles will be crucial and this layer could prevent lanthanide ions from leaching in aqueous environment based on coordination reaction (CQDs-EDTA-Eu). More importantly, the carboxyl-bearing surfaces would exhibit high solubility in water. The microstructures of CQDs-NH2 and CQDs-EDTA-Eu were explored by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2 A and B). The images revealed that the samples were composed of well-preserved and uniform spheres. The average diameters of the tiny granules were in the range between 2.5 and 3.4nm. No significant changes in the shapes or particle size were observed during the encapsulation of organic structure and europium ions.

FT-IR spectra were carried out for the investigation of the modification and coordination processes. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, the strong absorption bands 3437 and 1560cm−1 were corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of NH. The peak at 1407cm−1 would be caused by the stretching vibration of CN, suggesting that the carbon dots were terminated with amino groups. The absorption signals at 2982 and 2920cm−1 might be derived from stretching vibrations of methylene spacer in grafted ethylenediamine moiety. In the curve of CQDs-EDTA (Fig. 3A), the amide CO stretching vibration has been evidenced by the presence of peak at 1660cm−1. In addition, the two typical bands at 1631 and 1390cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetrical and symmetrical vibrations of carboxylate units. After the incorporation of europium ions, the chelation of the metallic ions has induced clear shifts of the respective asymmetrical and symmetrical vibration bands (from 1631 to 1568cm−1 and 1390 to 1418cm−1) (Fig. 3B). The CO vibration of the amide unit also shifted to the lower frequency (from 1660 to 1640cm−1).

The UV–vis spectra of DPA, CQDs-NH2, CQDs-EDTA-Eu and CQD-EDTA-Eu-DPA were recorded (Fig. 4 ). In the absorption curve of CQDs-NH2, the weak band at around 245nm is ascribed to π-π* transitions of CC groups. The broad band at 333nm will be attributed to trapping of excited state energy by the surface state. When the poly-dentated organic structure has been incorporated, new peaks at 271 and 279nm appear and the signals are derived from ethylenediaminetetraaceticdianhydride. It is obvious that the band at longer wavelength (333nm) increased substantially and moved to red side (345nm). The results revealed that the attached organic ligand may have influence on the surface defect states. In the sample of CQDs-EDTA-Eu-DPA, the well-defined absorption band at 273nm (mainly originated from DPA) improved significantly, indicating that 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid absorbed ultraviolet energy effectively in this hybrid carbon nanosystem.

CQDs-EDTA-Eu displayed a strong blue emission at 470nm before reacting with DPA and this band might be related to passivated surface defects of the carbon particles (Fig.S3). Upon the addition of DPA, the photoluminescence features change significantly and the emission peak evolves from blue area to red region (Fig. S4 and S5). Through complexation of the europium(III) ions with the oxygen and nitrogen atoms of functional units, DPA effectively sensitized the characteristic lanthanide emissions and intensive red luminescence has been achieved. The detailed fluorescence behavior of CQDs-EDTA-Eu solution (10μg/ml) in the presence of DPA has been studied by titration experiments. Under the irradiation at 282nm, the solution exhibits narrow emission bands which are in consistent with the deactivation of the Eu(III) excited states (5D0-7F1, 5D0-7F2 and 5D0-7F4). Distinguishable changes from blue to red could be observed with the naked eye under 254nm UV light. The quenching effect of CQDs-EDTA-Eu due to the presence of DPA is given in Fig. 5 . The emission intensity variation versus the concentration of DPA follows the simple linear equation y=15.06+125.26x (R2 =0.9953)(Fig. 6 ). The detection of limit (DL) was determined to be 137nM according to the equation DL=3×SD/slope, where SD is the standard deviation of the blank sample.

The specific sensing of cations is one of the primarily important aspects in materials chemistry. After the addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-EDTA-Eu-DPA decreased gradually (Fig. 8 ). The correlation between the emission change and the concentration of Cu2+ follows the simple linear equation y=87.50+12.776x (R2 =0.9858), (Fig. S6). The detection of limit (DL) was 80nM based on calculation through the equation DL=3×SD/slope, where SD is the standard deviation of the blank sample. As for the interference experiments, commonly used bio-relevant ions (K+, Na+, Ag+,Mg2+,Zn2+,Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cl−, SO4 2−) were explored. The achieved signal differences were much less than the effect of copper ions (Fig. S7). The above-mentioned fluorescence recovery and quenching behavior can be realized in terms of consecutive addition by DPA and Cu2+.

To further explore the selectivity of this novel system, the effects of multiple interference acids (phenylalanine, lysine, tyrosine, alanine, arginine, glycine, glutamine, tryptophan and ascorbic acid) on the emissions were studied (Fig.S9). The improvement of the red luminescence due to the titration of various species was virtually well within ±5%, suggesting that the current sensing motif provides favorable selectivity for the determination of GSH in the presence of common biological substances.

We have assembled a new on-off-on optical probe by encapsulation of both multi-dentated ligand and europium ions to the surface of carbon nanostructure. Initially, CQDs-EDTA-Eu exhibits blue luminescence, the presence of DPA resulted in a dramatic increase in the red emissions with a high selectivity toward DPA against reference species. This hybrid structure was successfully employed to the recognition of copper ions with a good “turn-off” effect and it can be used for the preparation of cation sensor. Subsequently, the red fluorescence could be recovered again in response to the addition of target thiols (GSH). It is anticipated that the on-off-onnanoprobe could be served as a diagnostic and monitoring material under biological and environmental conditions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Y.H. appreciates National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21371063), Science and Technology Project in Guangzhou (2014J4100054), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong (2015A030313378) and Guangdong Science and Technology plan (2013B010403025; 2016A050502053). J. W. thanks supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51571094), Guangdong province fund (2014B090915005) and Innovation team project by Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2017.07.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
